analysis:
• Markets price near-zero chance of ceasefire by month-end (2%) but a meaningful 40% by April 30 and 61% by June 30, implying traders see a 4-8 week window as the most likely resolution corridor — consistent with Rubio's "next couple weeks" comment on completing military objectives and Trump's April 6 deadline extension for Strait of Hormuz reopening.

• Houthi entry into the war today (first missile strike on Israel since Gaza ceasefire) is the major escalation catalyst, widening the conflict's geographic footprint and raising Red Sea shipping disruption risk on top of the existing Hormuz closure; Brent crude surged 4.2% to $112.57/bbl, its highest since July 2022, with analysts warning prices will keep climbing if Hormuz stays shut.

• Ceasefire talks remain stalled after Iran rejected Trump's 15-point plan on March 25 as "maximalist and unreasonable" and issued its own counterproposal demanding reparations and Hormuz sovereignty — the gap between positions is wide, which explains the sharp term structure: April 30 ceasefire at just 40% despite the conflict entering its second month with ~70% of Iran's military industry already targeted.

• Metaculus community forecast for "US-Iran ceasefire before May 2026" and Kalshi's April 30 contract (reportedly ~72%) both run hotter than Polymarket's 40% for the same date, suggesting Polymarket's crypto-native crowd is more skeptical of near-term resolution; insider trading concerns persist after suspicious account clusters bet ~$70K on March 31 ceasefire, potentially distorting short-dated contracts.

• CSIS, RAND, and Stimson Center analysts converge on a "battered but not broken" Iran outcome — the IDF estimates completing strikes on key military industry sites within days, but experts warn airpower alone won't produce decisive resolution, and the post-Khamenei leadership transition adds unpredictable political dynamics to any negotiated exit.

• Key catalysts to watch: Trump's April 6 deadline for Hormuz reopening (next major binary event), Pakistan-mediated backchannel talks, whether Houthi involvement triggers US strikes on Yemen, and Iran's diminishing missile inventory — with roughly one-third of its ~2,500 long-range missiles already destroyed, Tehran's leverage erodes weekly, which may ultimately force engagement with ceasefire terms.
